reasoning: Confirmed. In decryptKeyInfo() (xmlenc.js), line 245 reads keyRetrievalMethodUri from keyRetrievalMethod.getAttribute('URI') — a raw XML attribute value from attacker-supplied XML. Lines 246 and 273 splice keyRetrievalMethodUri.substring(1) directly into XPath string literals using single-quote delimiters with no escaping. substring(1) only strips the leading '#'; it does not remove single quotes or other XPath metacharacters. An attacker supplying a URI like '#foo\' or \'1\'=\'1' causes immediate string-context escape and arbitrary XPath predicate injection. The RetrievalMethod code path is taken whenever the inline EncryptedKey child is absent from KeyInfo (line 241) — no non-default option required. No Zod/Joi/AJV or any schema validation is applied to the XML before parsing. The xpath library supports safe parameterized $var bindings but they are not used here. The decrypt() entry point (line 190) accepts raw XML from any caller — in SAML and similar flows this is fully attacker-controlled with no authentication barrier. The finding mislabels the file as constants.js (the actual file is xmlenc.js) but the line numbers (246, 273) and code description are accurate in all other respects.
